claim: UNFPA reported in February 2026 that Viet Nam has limited access to integrated long-term care, that 99 percent of older people depend on family care, and that women provide 72 percent of reported care responsibility.

source.title: Population Ageing in Viet Nam: From Demographic Transition to Development Opportunity

method: Reviewed the dated UNFPA Viet Nam story sections on long-term care, family dependence, unpaid care responsibility, healthy life expectancy, and policy priorities.
limitations: The page summarizes UNFPA analysis but does not expose the underlying survey tables, sampling frame, or definitions for the 99 percent and 72 percent estimates.
National framing cannot identify households with distant family, functional need, or no available local responder.
Dependence on family care does not prove that family care is absent, inadequate, or unwanted in a specific household.

claim: UNFPA reported that a six-month integrated-care pilot across five provinces and Da Nang screened about 1,500 older people, trained more than 1,000 staff and home caregivers, and produced care plans, operating procedures, caregiver guidance, and care-management software.

source.title: UNFPA’s interventions towards the development of a care ecosystem for older persons in Vietnam

method: Reviewed the UNFPA program description, pilot locations, reported participant counts, care products, national context estimates, and stated need for further evidence.
limitations: Program counts are reported by an implementing institution and are not an independent outcome evaluation.
The page does not provide a comparison group, attrition data, response-time data, cost per participant, or clinical outcomes.
The national functional-limitation estimates refer to 2021 and do not measure family travel time or local responder availability.

claim: WHO's Integrated Care for Older People guideline recommends person-centred, coordinated community care and organizes evidence around mobility and vitality, sensory capacity, cognition and psychological well-being, urinary incontinence, falls, and caregiver support.

source.title: Integrated care for older people: guidelines on community-level interventions to manage declines in intrinsic capacity

method: Reviewed the WHO publication overview, evidence-resource description, priority actions, and linked person-centred assessment and implementation materials.
limitations: Global clinical guidance is not evidence that a specific Vietnamese service can deliver the recommended pathways.
The guideline does not establish the efficacy of a family dashboard, wearable, or remote-response business model.
Local adaptation requires workforce, referral, financing, language, consent, and service-capacity assessment.

claim: The World Bank and JICA reported in 2021 that Viet Nam became an ageing society in 2015, projected it to become an aged society by 2035, and identified development of a long-term-care system as a required reform.

source.title: Vietnam: Adapting to An Aging Society

method: Reviewed the publication metadata and key findings on demographic transition, fiscal constraints, social protection, and long-term-care reform.
limitations: The demographic projection predates newer UNFPA and national estimates and should not be treated as a current population count.
National policy analysis does not measure household-level distance, care quality, or service willingness to pay.
A need for long-term-care reform does not identify which delivery model is effective.

claim: A 2024 World Bank field report documented 186 supported intergenerational self-help clubs across six Vietnamese provinces, reporting more than 24,725 beneficiaries and services including home visits, medication support, health monitoring, social participation, and assistance with daily activities.

source.title: How Intergenerational Self-Help Clubs Transform Elderly Care in Viet Nam

method: Reviewed the World Bank story highlights, stated project reach, club structure, service descriptions, implementation geography, and participant examples.
limitations: This is a project feature story, not an independent impact evaluation.
Beneficiary and club counts do not prove reliable coverage, response closure, cost-effectiveness, or health benefit.
The six-province implementation cannot establish national or Ho Chi Minh City service capacity.
Participant testimony is illustrative and cannot estimate average effect.

claim: Viet Nam's Prime Minister issued Decision 1648/QD-TTg on 1 August 2025 approving a programme to expand intergenerational self-help clubs through 2035.

source.title: Quyết định số 1648/QĐ-TTg của Thủ tướng Chính phủ: Phê duyệt Đề án nhân rộng câu lạc bộ liên thế hệ tự giúp nhau đến năm 2035

method: Reviewed the official government record for decision number, issuing authority, issue date, signer, title, and attached signed document.
limitations: Approval of an expansion programme does not prove implementation, coverage, staffing, service quality, or availability in a target commune.
The record does not establish that clubs can provide clinical or emergency response.
Any operational use requires verification of the signed decision, local implementation instruments, and current commune-level capacity.

claim: The completed 2019 Viet Nam Population and Housing Census provides national and subnational tabulations on population structure, migration, disability status, housing, and household living conditions that can define the eligible older-person population and household context.

source.title: The 2019 Population and Housing Census Data Dashboard

method: Reviewed the General Statistics Office dashboard description for population structure, migration, housing, household living conditions, geographic stratification, and downloadable census tables.
limitations: Public tabulations do not establish how quickly a family member can travel to an older person or whether that person is willing and able to respond.
Census household composition is not a measure of functional need, current care receipt, consent, or local responder availability.
The reference date is 1 April 2019, so current service planning requires a newer population frame or explicit temporal adjustment.

claim: The World Bank and General Statistics Office Time-Use Survey 2022 provides a public-use, multi-stage sample covering six Vietnamese socio-economic regions and measures unpaid care time, including care of elders and children, among respondents aged 15 to 64.
method: Reviewed the World Bank Microdata Library metadata for study ID, universe, six-region coverage, field dates, multi-stage sampling, unpaid elder-care measures, public-use access, and producer attribution.
limitations: The respondent universe excludes people over 64, so this source measures caregiver time rather than older-person care need or older-person preferences.
Time spent caring does not establish travel time, response timeliness, care quality, resolution, or clinical outcome.
The dataset is region-level in the public metadata; commune-level response-network mapping cannot be inferred without inspecting the released files and geography controls.

claim: The National Survey on People with Disabilities 2016 is an official General Statistics Office survey report that can inform functional-limitation and disability measurement for Vietnamese population subgroups, subject to checking its older-person and geographic tabulations.

source.title: Vietnam National Survey on People with Disabilities 2016

method: Reviewed the official General Statistics Office landing record and linked survey provenance, then classified the source as a functional-need input only; the exact report release date is not exposed, so the survey year is used.
limitations: Disability measures are not interchangeable with all functional-care needs, frailty, cognitive decline, or short-term assistance requirements.
The 2016 reference period is stale for current service allocation unless reweighted or paired with newer population and disability evidence.
The report does not establish family availability, consent, local responder capacity, or whether a limitation generated an unresolved care request.

claim: The 2015 National Internal Migration Survey provides official evidence on internal-migration patterns and migrant access to services, making it a context source for identifying where family separation may require direct measurement rather than an assumed care gap.
method: Reviewed the UN Viet Nam publication record for reference period, publication date, GSO authorship, stated policy use, and the survey's focus on internal migrants and service access; classified it as migration-context evidence rather than a care-response measure.
limitations: Migration status or destination does not identify an older person's actual family travel time, contact frequency, or emergency availability.
The 2015 reference period predates newer census and intercensal migration evidence.
The source cannot establish functional need, consent, local responder presence, response closure, or health outcomes.

claim: The General Statistics Office analysis of the 2021 Population Change and Family Planning Survey includes a new older-person module covering care needs and situation, making it the strongest identified candidate for testing older-person care-related measures before attempting a care-gap denominator.

source.title: Older Persons in Viet Nam: An analysis of the Population Change and Family Planning Survey 2021

method: Reviewed the official publication metadata and description for survey date, GSO authorship, national indicator purpose, and the first-time older-person care-needs module; classified it as a candidate source requiring questionnaire and tabulation inspection.
limitations: The publication description does not by itself reveal whether family travel time, consent preference, responder availability, or event-level resolution are measured.
A care-needs module can support prevalence or stratification but cannot prove that a local response network is reachable or effective.
Microdata access, sampling weights, missingness, and geographic release must be verified before using the source in a denominator.
